lastUpdateSummary.headline: Selective Hormuz passage is widening a little, but only under tightly managed and still dangerous conditions.
lastUpdateSummary.body: A fresh Lloyd's List read of the 16 March Hormuz cluster shows Karachi was not an isolated anomaly: the India-flagged LPG carriers Shivalik and Nanda Devi and the Pakistan-owned crude tanker Karachi all made eastbound passages in recent days, with Lloyd's saying some outbound ships routed around Larak Island inside Iranian territorial waters. But the same Lloyd's package says crews and insurers still refuse to treat the corridor as reopened, with seven Dynacom tankers stuck, one attempted exit reportedly turned back after an Iranian challenge, and passage decisions still being made ship by ship rather than by any blanket nationality-based clearance.

last24hSummary.headline: The latest 24-hour picture is a controlled trickle through Hormuz layered on top of persistent crew, insurance and interference risk.
last24hSummary.body: Across MarineTraffic, Kpler, Windward, CNBC and newly opened Lloyd's List article pages, the best evidence still points to selective negotiated movement rather than normal market reopening. Fresh Lloyd's reporting adds vessel-level color: Shivalik, Nanda Devi and Karachi all transited eastbound; the Dynacom-operated Smyrni appears to have crossed after loading Saudi crude for India with AIS interruptions and GNSS interference; and owners still describe transits as case-by-case permissions in a corridor where insurers remain hesitant and crews are reluctant to gamble despite high freight incentives.

vesselAttacks24hSummary.headline: No credible fresh vessel attacks were confirmed in the last 24 hours around the Persian Gulf and Strait of Hormuz.
vesselAttacks24hSummary.body: This run explicitly checked TankerTrackers, MarineTraffic, Kpler and staunovo on X; Lloyd's List's Hormuz crisis and Daily Briefing result sets, with relevant 16-17 March Lloyd's article pages opened directly; Windward's 16 March maritime intelligence daily; CNBC's Bessent interview; and fresh web research on vessel attacks, including follow-up reporting on the 12 March attack on the Thai cargo ship Mayuree Naree. The result was no credible newly reported 24-hour vessel strike, boarding or mining incident in or immediately around Hormuz. The newest attack-related color was follow-up only: 20 Mayuree Naree crew returned to Thailand while three remained unaccounted for after the earlier 12 March attack; Lloyd's still describes ships as moving only case by case after the 11-12 March attack wave; Kpler and Lloyd's both continue to flag GPS/GNSS disruption and AIS reliability problems; and Lloyd's notes one Dynacom tanker crew reportedly tried to leave but turned back after an Iranian challenge. So the operational risk picture remains severe, but this run did not surface a fresh verified vessel attack inside the last 24 hours.

previousDaySummary.headline: March 16 showed a politically managed trickle through Hormuz, not a return to normal traffic.
previousDaySummary.body: The day began with the operating picture still dominated by extreme scarcity of visible movement: Windward's 16 March update recorded only three outbound Hormuz crossings and no inbound transits on 15 March, including the sanctioned VLCC NORA after loading about 2.01 million barrels at Kharg for China. Broader route-capacity reporting still put effective Middle East exports at only about one-third of normal levels despite record use of alternative outlets, with roughly 350 tankers stranded, Saudi western-port exports reaching 5.9 million barrels per day on 9 March and Fujairah's crude bypass capacity still limited to roughly 1.5-1.8 million barrels per day. Later on 16 March, MarineTraffic and Kpler flagged the Aframax Karachi carrying Das crude as the first non-Iranian cargo tanker seen clearing Hormuz with AIS active since the crisis escalated, while CNBC reported Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent saying Washington is tolerating ongoing Iranian oil tanker departures and that India is still seeking clearance for 22 additional ships. The combined signal from the day was that some cargoes can move, but only through selective diplomatic or political permission layered on top of a system that remains far from commercially normalized across Hormuz, Kharg, Yanbu, Fujairah and the waiting fleet offshore.

sources.name: Lloyd's List Hormuz Crisis
sources.collectionRule: Open every relevant result from this search page, read each article page itself, and capture all new information and especially all exposed data points before deciding whether to promote or skip it. Do not treat the search page teaser text as sufficient.

sources.name: Fresh web research: Hormuz shadow fleet and vessel attacks
Fresh web research: Red Sea Yanbu Fujairah Hormuz bypass flows and vessel attacks

items.title: MarineTraffic says the Aframax Karachi became the first non-Iranian cargo tanker seen clearing Hormuz with AIS still active since the crisis escalated
items.summary: MarineTraffic says the 237-metre Aframax Karachi, carrying Abu Dhabi's Das crude for Pakistan, entered Iran's Exclusive Economic Zone at 11:33 UTC on 15 March, crossed the Strait of Hormuz at 14:43 UTC and then continued through the Gulf of Oman at about 9.6 knots with AIS still on. The post also says more than 20 long-range tankers have now exited the Gulf. For the dashboard, the useful point is that movement is no longer completely frozen, but the first openly visible non-Iranian cargo transit appears to have happened under a selective and tightly managed routing pattern rather than under any broad restoration of normal commercial passage.
items.figureNote: Key figures: Karachi is a 237-metre Aframax; entered Iran's EEZ at 11:33 UTC on 15 March; crossed Hormuz at 14:43 UTC; continued at about 9.6 knots; MarineTraffic also says more than 20 long-range tankers have exited the Gulf.

items.title: A second Fujairah disruption briefly halted oil loadings at one of the UAE’s main Hormuz bypass outlets
items.sourceName: Fresh web research: Red Sea Yanbu Fujairah Hormuz bypass flows
items.summary: Reuters-reported coverage says oil loading operations at Fujairah were suspended on 16 March after another drone attack triggered fires in the petroleum industrial zone. Fujairah typically handles about 1 million barrels per day of the UAE’s Murban crude and had only just resumed operations after a previous strike, so the new halt matters because it shows that relief routes outside Hormuz are now directly exposed to repeated attack and cannot be treated as stable spare capacity.
items.figureNote: The key operational figure is roughly 1 million barrels per day of Murban crude typically moving via Fujairah, one of the UAE’s few export outlets outside Hormuz.

items.title: Japan is not yet planning a Hormuz escort mission despite heavy Middle East energy dependence
items.sourceName: Fresh web research: hormuz shadow fleet
items.summary: Reuters-reported coverage says Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi told parliament that Japan has made no decision to dispatch escort ships and is still examining what is possible within its legal framework. For the dashboard, that matters because Japan is one of the large Asian importers most exposed to Hormuz disruption, and its reluctance to commit naval cover suggests that any escort-led normalization in compliant tanker and gas-carrier traffic will remain limited even among states with strong energy incentives to reopen the route.
items.figureNote: The practical signal is that a major Hormuz-dependent importer is still not committing escort vessels, which keeps the ceiling low for any near-term coalition-backed recovery in compliant traffic.

items.title: Windward says Hormuz managed only three outbound crossings on 15 March while Kharg-linked liftings and regional congestion kept building
items.summary: Windward’s 16 March maritime intelligence update says only three outbound vessels and no inbound transits were recorded through Hormuz on 15 March, with one of the crossings being the sanctioned VLCC NORA after loading about 2.01 million barrels at Kharg and heading for Ningbo. The same update puts confirmed maritime-security incidents since the start of the war at 20, shows weekly tanker port calls at Saudi Red Sea ports rising from about 58 in late December to more than 90 by early March, and flags worsening transshipment pressure at Salalah and Karachi. For the dashboard, the useful point is that the system is not simply shut: a small number of politically tolerable or sanctioned cargoes still move, while the workaround network outside Hormuz is getting more congested and operationally important.
items.figureNote: Key figures: 3 outbound and 0 inbound Hormuz crossings on 15 March; NORA loaded about 2.01 million barrels at Kharg; 20 confirmed maritime-security incidents since 28 February; Saudi Red Sea tanker port calls rose from roughly 58 in late December 2025 to more than 90 by early March 2026.

items.title: Record Yanbu and Fujairah use is still leaving effective Middle East exports at only about one-third of normal levels
items.sourceName: Fresh web research: Red Sea Yanbu Fujairah Hormuz bypass flows
items.summary: Fresh reporting citing the IEA and Kpler says Hormuz workarounds remain materially inadequate even after record diversion use. The piece says about 350 oil tankers are currently stranded in the area, only around 80 ships have transited the strait since the war began on 28 February, Saudi western-port exports hit a record 5.9 million barrels per day on 9 March with East-West pipeline capacity heading toward 7 million barrels per day, and Fujairah can raise crude throughput only from about 1.5 to 1.8 million barrels per day. For the dashboard, the important point is that bypass infrastructure is helping but still cannot absorb the lost system volume, so regional flow stress remains spread across Yanbu, Fujairah, waiting fleets and long-haul replacement sourcing rather than easing cleanly.
items.figureNote: Key figures: effective Middle Eastern exports at about one-third of normal levels; around 350 oil tankers stranded; only about 80 ships through Hormuz since 28 February; Saudi western-port exports at 5.9 mb/d on 9 March with 7 mb/d pipeline capacity in sight; Fujairah crude capacity around 1.5-1.8 mb/d.

items.title: Lloyd's List says Indian and Pakistan state-linked ships are now among the few eastbound vessels transiting Hormuz
items.summary: Lloyd's List reports that the India-flagged LPG carriers Shivalik and Nanda Devi and the Pakistan-owned crude tanker Karachi all transited the Strait of Hormuz around 13-15 March, with all three voyages eastbound. The article adds useful route color beyond the earlier Karachi post: Karachi and the bulker Minoan Sky reportedly routed around Larak Island inside Iranian territorial waters, Nanda Devi's patchy AIS also suggests a Larak loop, and the previously attacked Star Gwyneth may have hugged the Iranian shoreline as well. For the dashboard, the important read is that some non-Iranian traffic is getting through, but by visibly cautious routing and still within a transit pattern dominated by Iranian-, Greek- and Chinese-linked tonnage rather than by a broad reopening.
items.figureNote: Lloyd's says vessels linked to Iran, Greece and China still accounted for about 47%, 17% and 9% of all transits respectively, while the newly noted India/Pakistan passages were all eastbound between roughly 13 and 15 March.

items.title: Lloyd's List says crews and insurers still see Hormuz as too dangerous for any normal resumption of trade
items.summary: Lloyd's List reports that most vessels trapped in the Gulf are still not moving, with one owner quoted saying there is still no insurance and no crew agreement to run the strait routinely. The piece says seven Dynacom tankers remain among the trapped fleet, at least 11 seafarers have been killed in Iranian rocket and drone attacks during the crisis, and one Dynacom crew reportedly tried to exit but turned back after being challenged by Iranian forces. For the dashboard, this matters because it sharpens the difference between a few politically tolerated passages and any true normalization: even where cargo economics are attractive, crew consent, war-risk cover and Iranian control of who can pass remain binding constraints.
items.figureNote: Useful hard color: seven Dynacom tankers are cited as trapped in the Gulf and Lloyd's says at least 11 seafarers have been killed in Iranian rocket and drone attacks during the crisis.

items.title: Storage saturation is spreading from Saudi and Kuwaiti terminals into confirmed Gulf production cuts
items.summary: Kim BENNI says Ras Tanura and Ju'aymah were already about 70% full as of two days ago, with Kuwait showing the same pattern, and argues that further Saudi and Kuwaiti production cuts are likely. The same post says Qatar, the UAE and Iraq are no longer building inventories because production cuts there are already confirmed, with the Middle East as a whole having added 26.7 million barrels. For the dashboard, the useful point is that storage utilisation is becoming a live operational constraint across multiple exporters, which helps explain why low crossing counts are now feeding directly into upstream output losses rather than just voyage delays.
items.figureNote: The main figures are about 70% storage utilisation at Ras Tanura and Ju'aymah two days ago and a cumulative 26.7 million-barrel inventory build across the Middle East.

items.title: Kharg inventories are rising quickly even while tankers remain visible at the berths
items.summary: Kim BENNI says Iran added 4.33 million barrels of inland crude inventory week on week, including 3.2 million barrels in just two days on Kharg Island. The post says ships remain visible at the Kharg berths, but low flaring suggests production is below normal and the fast stock build implies a mismatch between upstream supply and export clearance. For the dashboard, that matters because visible loading activity at Kharg may now overstate effective flow throughput if inland tanks are filling faster than cargoes are leaving.
items.figureNote: The key figures are a 4.33 million-barrel week-on-week rise in inland Iranian inventories and a 3.2 million-barrel increase on Kharg in just two days, despite tankers remaining visible at the berths.

items.title: Another 2 million barrels of Iranian crude departed while visible Hormuz traffic stayed subdued
items.summary: TankerTrackers says another 2 million barrels of Iranian crude departed on 15 March while the Strait of Hormuz remained very quiet. For the dashboard, the operational point is that Iranian-linked export flow is still clearing cargo in meaningful increments even when visible commercial transit remains thin, which means low crossing counts can still understate residual throughput tied to sanctioned or selectively tolerated movements.
items.figureNote: The key hard figure is the additional 2 million barrels of Iranian crude reported as departed on 15 March despite still-quiet visible traffic through Hormuz.

items.title: Storage limits and bypass bottlenecks are becoming a bigger constraint than passage alone
items.summary: The Guardian reports that as Hormuz disruption persists, Gulf producers are running harder on bypass routes but still nearing storage limits that could force more shut-ins. It says Saudi pipeline exports to Yanbu have doubled to about 3 million barrels per day with a target of 5 million, UAE pipeline crude to Fujairah has climbed to about 1.6 million barrels per day, and at least 25 large tankers are already heading to Yanbu, yet analysts still estimate only about seven days of storage for Aramco and less than 20 days for the UAE. For the dashboard, the important point is that weak crossings now understate total system stress because barrels can remain trapped upstream even when some alternative loadings continue outside the strait itself.
items.figureNote: The key operational figures are roughly 3 million barrels per day already moving by Saudi pipeline to Yanbu, about 1.6 million barrels per day via the UAE pipeline to Fujairah, at least 25 tankers heading for Yanbu, and only days to weeks of spare storage for major Gulf producers if the strait remains constrained.

items.title: Zero AIS-confirmed crossings are now coinciding with a large waiting fleet and continued Kharg liftings
items.summary: Windward’s 15 March maritime intelligence update says 14 March was the first full day of the conflict with no AIS-confirmed crossings through Hormuz in either direction, while remote-sensing still detected roughly 400 vessels operating in the Gulf of Oman and at least eight vessels positioned east of the strait. It also says Kharg remained active, with the sanctioned tankers SERENA and ARK III departing after 11 March carrying a combined estimated 2.68 million barrels, and satellite imagery showing a heavy tanker presence around the terminal. For the dashboard, the useful point is that a zero-crossing day no longer means the wider system is empty: it can reflect a combination of waiting tonnage, lower-visibility movement and resilient Iranian-linked export handling at Kharg.
items.figureNote: The key operational figures are zero AIS-confirmed crossings on 14 March, about 400 vessels in the Gulf of Oman, at least eight vessels positioned east of the strait, and an estimated 2.68 million barrels lifted by SERENA and ARK III from Kharg after 11 March.

items.title: Selective clearances and dark-fleet movements are keeping a narrow flow alive through Hormuz
items.summary: The Maritime Executive reports that Iranian or Iranian-approved ships are still making use of Hormuz while most other traffic remains effectively choked off. It names the Indian-flagged LPG carriers Shivalik and Nanda Devi as approved passages, identifies the Saudi-laden VLCC Smyrni and sanctioned product tankers Ocean Guardian and Blooming Dale among vessels that appear to have transited, and says Lloyd’s List Intelligence has detected nine dark-fleet transits in March while assessing about 45 in total. For the dashboard, the useful point is that passage is increasingly being rationed by political approval and low-visibility tactics rather than restored on a broad commercial basis.
items.figureNote: The key hard figures are nine dark-fleet Hormuz transits detected in March versus an estimated total of about 45, plus the named examples of approved Indian LPG passages and sanctioned product-tanker movements.

items.title: Kpler
items.summary: Kpler says Southeast Asia previously depended on roughly 1.8 million barrels per day of crude and about 500 thousand barrels per day of refined products moving through Hormuz, and that replacement barrels from the US, Nigeria, Australia and Brazil are not enough. This matters for the dashboard because it shows the effect of reduced crossings is not only local tanker scarcity but also downstream refinery pressure and inventory drawdowns in end markets.
items.figureNote: The useful number here is the lost inflow into Southeast Asia, which turns a crossings story into a demand-side stress story.

items.title: TankerTrackers.com, Inc.
items.summary: TankerTrackers says that after the previous night's US strikes on Kharg Island, all 55 crude storage tanks appeared intact and two Iranian NITC tankers began loading a combined 2.7 million barrels of crude on the morning of 14 March. That is a strong indication that Iranian export infrastructure and state-linked tanker liftings remained operational even while broader Gulf traffic stayed disrupted.
items.figureNote: The most useful hard figure here is the 2.7 million barrels loading across two NITC tankers, which helps explain why Iranian-linked flows can stay resilient while compliant traffic remains thin.

items.title: Lloyd's List Hormuz Crisis
items.summary: Lloyd's List reports that about half of tanker and gas-carrier transits above 10,000 dwt between 1 and 8 March were shadow-fleet vessels, including suspected dark transits. The same piece notes heavier AIS-off behavior, widespread GNSS interference, and renewed loading activity at Jask south of Hormuz, which is important because Jask offers Iran a slower but meaningful bypass to the usual Kharg export pattern.
items.figureNote: Jask loadings are operationally slow, but their reappearance is a specific clue for interpreting eastbound tanker movements near southern Iran.

items.title: Kpler Blog
items.summary: Kpler says tanker transits through Hormuz have fallen by roughly 92% compared with the week before the conflict, and argues that the key constraint is security risk rather than a total disappearance of insurance quotes. Its chartering view is useful because it explains why even owners who could technically transit may still hold back tonnage once war-risk premiums, rerouting costs, compliance checks and strike risk are priced together.
items.figureNote: The headline number is the 92% collapse in tanker transits versus the prior week, paired with large volumes of trapped or idle Gulf tonnage.

items.title: Fresh web research: Hormuz shadow fleet and vessel attacks
items.summary: Recent reporting cited in the research pass says multiple vessels have been struck within a 48-hour period and that GPS spoofing is compounding navigational uncertainty across the strait and wider Gulf. This is useful context for the dashboard because it implies that low observed traffic can reflect both real hesitation and degraded positional visibility, especially when vessels wait offshore, switch off AIS, or transit under heavy interference.
items.figureNote: The operational takeaway is that crossing counts should be read alongside interference and waiting-fleet conditions, not as a perfect census of all movement.

items.title: India-operated LPG carriers clear Hormuz under rare Iran-approved arrangement
items.summary: gCaptain, citing Reuters reporting, says the LPG carriers Shivalik and Nanda Devi, both operated by Shipping Corporation of India, were allowed through Hormuz after high-level India-Iran contacts. That is useful for the dashboard because it suggests passage is no longer simply open or shut: selected cargoes can still move when nationality, operator profile and diplomatic leverage line up, implying that future counts may hinge on negotiated access rather than a broad return of commercial confidence.
items.figureNote: India consumes more than 30 million tonnes of cooking gas a year, with about 60% imported and roughly 90% of those imports sourced from the Middle East, which helps explain why even a small number of cleared LPG passages can matter operationally.

items.title: Escort and maritime-insurance plans move closer to being active routing variables
items.summary: Riviera reports that the US administration is explicitly pairing possible naval escorts with maritime-insurance support for Hormuz transits, while Bimco argues escorts could reduce threat for some ships but cannot realistically cover all tanker demand in the current threat environment. For the dashboard, the main implication is that any recovery in compliant traffic may arrive unevenly by operator and risk appetite, with state-backed cover helping a subset of voyages rather than restoring normal volumes across the board.
items.figureNote: The operational point is not a single transit count but the widening split between ships that may re-enter under escort or public backstop and the larger pool of owners still likely to stand off outside the Gulf.

items.title: India’s cleared LPG passage comes with a detailed picture of the waiting queue west of Hormuz
items.summary: The Hindu reports that after Shivalik and Nanda Devi crossed Hormuz, 22 Indian-flagged vessels with 611 seafarers remained on standby west of the strait, including six LPG carriers, one LNG tanker, four crude tankers and one chemical-products tanker. It also says Nanda Devi crossed during the day with AIS transmitting, which is useful for the dashboard because it suggests some politically cleared voyages may move openly rather than as dark transits, while the composition of the remaining queue gives a concrete read on how much India-linked energy tonnage is still waiting for permission or risk conditions to change.
items.figureNote: The most useful hard figures are the 22 Indian-flagged vessels still waiting west of Hormuz and their cargo mix, because that turns a one-off passage story into a measurable pipeline of still-delayed energy traffic.

items.title: MarineTraffic
items.summary: MarineTraffic says only 15 laden liquid tankers crossed the Strait of Hormuz between 28 February and 12 March. It also highlights the case of Wen Yao, described as maintaining AIS transmission inside the Gulf despite links to restricted trade, which matters because it contrasts with the more typical shadow-fleet pattern of going dark during sensitive voyages.
items.figureNote: The useful quantitative anchor here is the 15-laden-tanker count over the period, which gives a very sparse baseline for comparing dashboard-observed crossings.

items.title: Lloyd's List Daily Briefing
items.summary: The latest visible Lloyd's List briefing cluster around Hormuz is focused less on reopening optimism and more on secondary effects: liner detours, insurance stress, and owners refusing highly profitable voyages because the security calculus still looks unacceptable. That broad framing supports a dashboard interpretation in which low crossings are not a temporary data glitch but part of a wider commercial stand-off.
